title: NOC 94101 – Foundry workers | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s foundry industry remains a critical pillar of the automotive, aerospace, and construction sectors. Foundry Workers (NOC 94101) are the skilled hands that transform molten metal into high-precision components. As the industry integrates greener melting technologies and advanced casting methods, these roles offer a stable, essential-service career path with significant opportunities for international workers to settle in Canada’s industrial heartlands.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Processing and manufacturing machine operatorsSub-Major Group941 – Machine operators in mineral and metal processing

duties: Mouldmaking: Making and repairing sand moulds by hand or using automated moulding machines and patterns.
Coremaking: Creating sand or ceramic cores to form internal holes or void spaces within metal castings.
Furnace Operation: Operating electric, induction, or gas furnaces to melt metals for casting.
Casting: Pouring molten metal into moulds using hand ladles or by operating specialized casting machines.
Finishing: Monitoring the cooling process and ensuring castings meet precise engineering specifications.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is required.
Experience: Several weeks to several months of on-the-job training are typically provided.
Specialized Training: For manual mouldmakers, up to two years of on-the-job training or an apprenticeship may be required.
Certifications: WHMIS 2026 (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) and specific safety training for high-heat environments are mandatory.
salary_prospects: Note: High-seniority roles in specialized aerospace foundries in Quebec can reach upwards of $40.00/hr.
job_outlook: While automation is optimizing the casting process, the need for human oversight in precision inspection and complex manual mouldmaking remains high. The 10-year outlook is "Balanced," with the majority of openings stemming from a massive wave of retirements in the primary metal manufacturing sector.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for foundry workers is driven by a high replacement need for retiring workers and steady growth in the specialized manufacturing sector:
Ontario (Hamilton & GTA): Moderate demand as the province’s "Battery Belt" and automotive assembly lines require a constant supply of cast components.
Quebec: Stable to Good prospects in the Montreal and Saguenay regions, particularly for foundries serving the aerospace and primary metal industries.
British Columbia: Limited but steady demand within specialized marine and industrial foundries.
National Trend: Over 95% of the workforce is currently male, but there is an aggressive 2026 initiative to recruit diverse talent into these high-paying technical roles.
Why Work in Canada as a Foundry Worker?
Essential Infrastructure Role: Foundry workers support the manufacturing of everything from municipal water pipes to aircraft engines, ensuring high job security.
Technological Shift: Gain experience with induction furnaces, 3D-printed sand moulds, and cleaner, low-emission casting processes.
Path to Higher TEER Roles: Proven experience often leads to promotion into NOC 92010 (Supervisors) or NOC 93100 (Central Control Operators), which are higher-tier PR qualifiers.
Stable Earnings: These roles often come with strong union representation and comprehensive medical benefits that exceed the manufacturing average.
pathways: Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP): This is the strongest route for TEER 4 workers. The Ontario In-Demand Skills Stream and the Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot are prime targets.

Express Entry (Trade Category): In 2026, IRCC has renewed the Trade Occupations category. While NOC 94101 is TEER 4, workers who advance to "Setup" or "Lead Hand" roles may qualify for category-based draws.
AIP (Atlantic Immigration Program): A direct PR pathway for those with a job offer from a designated employer in Atlantic Canada.
TR to PR: If you have 12 months of Canadian work experience, you may qualify for specific localized transition programs updated for 2026.
